topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my phone screen, a familiar, unwelcome chime. It was a photo of me, taken at the school fair, captioned with a slightly patronizing comment about my 'enthusiasm for history.' My stomach tightened, a knot forming just beneath my ribs. Mr. Harrison. He was always so composed in class, a pillar of academic authority. Now, he was documenting my existence, sharing snippets of my life as if he owned the right to curate it. I scrolled through the feed, the image of his perfectly tailored jacket next to my slightly blurry picture, and a hot flush crept up my neck. Why was he doing this? It felt like a spotlight being shone directly onto my awkward adolescence, a constant, low-grade irritation that settled deep in my chest. I quickly muted the notifications, my thumb hovering over the screen, trying to physically push the intrusion away.

[story 2]

I found the post tucked away in my feed, and the air in my cramped dorm room suddenly felt thick and stale. It was a candid shot—me laughing, something genuinely happy, and Mr. Harrison’s profile picture staring back at me from the corner. A sharp, involuntary sigh escaped my lips. I stared at the screen for a long moment, my jaw clenching so tightly my molars ached. It wasn't just the observation; it was the implication. He was watching, cataloging. Every casual interaction, every moment of vulnerability, was being logged and broadcast by the man who was supposed to be guiding my education. I tossed the phone onto the desk, the clatter echoing in the quiet room. I stood up, pacing the small space, my movements jerky and agitated, needing to burn off the sudden, prickly heat rising in my cheeks.

[story 3]

When I saw the update pop up—a picture of me looking stressed over a particularly difficult essay, tagged by Mr. Harrison—a cold wave washed over me. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I felt a distinct hardening in my chest, a refusal to engage. He seemed to think he had some kind of special access, some proprietary right to observe my struggles. I didn't want him to see the messy parts, the parts where I genuinely struggled to grasp the material. I navigated to his profile, my fingers trembling slightly as I read the carefully chosen caption. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, laced with a bitter edge. I closed the app without replying, the silence of my room amplifying the sour taste in my mouth. It felt like a violation, a constant, unwanted scrutiny that made every future interaction with him feel strained and performative.
